title: Reality Czech
description: A practical path into Czech through real language, short explanations, and immediate practice.
license: Adapted curriculum content is attributed to Reality Czech and distributed under CC BY-SA where that license applies. Third-party media retains its original license.

units.title: Unit 1 — First Czech
units.description: Pronunciation, greetings, noun gender, být, and numbers.

lessons.title: Sounds and spelling
lessons.summary: Learn the most important relationships between Czech spelling and pronunciation.
objectives: Distinguish c, č, s, and š

sections.heading: Long vowels
sections.body: An acute accent usually marks a long vowel. Length can distinguish words, so do not treat the accent as decoration.

sections.heading: Useful consonants
sections.body: Czech spelling is comparatively regular. Learn each marked consonant as its own sound.

vocabulary.note: Č begins with a ch-like sound.
Ů is a long u sound.

quiz.prompt: Which letter sounds most like English sh?

lessons.title: Greetings and introductions
lessons.summary: Use simple greetings and say who you are.
objectives: Choose an informal or formal greeting

sections.heading: Hello
sections.body: Ahoj is informal. Dobrý den is the dependable formal or neutral greeting used with strangers and in shops or offices.

sections.heading: Names
sections.body: Jmenuju se… is a common spoken way to say your name. Jak se jmenujete? is the polite question.

quiz.prompt: What is the safest greeting when entering an office?
quiz.explanation: Dobrý den is appropriate in formal and neutral daytime situations.

lessons.title: Nouns and gender
lessons.summary: Recognize the three grammatical genders and begin learning nouns with useful cues.
objectives: Use common noun endings as clues
Avoid treating gender as biological sex

sections.heading: Three genders
sections.body: Czech nouns are masculine, feminine, or neuter. Gender controls agreement and later affects case endings.

sections.heading: Useful clues
sections.body: Many feminine nouns end in -a and many neuter nouns end in -o or -e. Masculine nouns often end in a consonant, but exceptions are normal.

quiz.explanation: Auto ends in -o and is neuter: to auto.

lessons.title: The verb být
lessons.summary: Use the present tense of být, the Czech verb ‘to be.’

sections.heading: Present tense
sections.body: Czech verbs identify the person, so subject pronouns are often omitted unless they are emphasized or contrasted.

sections.heading: Simple statements
sections.body: Use být to identify people and things or describe their basic state.

quiz.explanation: My means we, so the form is jsme.

lessons.title: Numbers 0–20
lessons.summary: Build the number vocabulary needed for prices, ages, addresses, and telephone numbers.

sections.heading: Core numbers
sections.body: Learn numbers in small groups and say them aloud. Czech telephone numbers are commonly read in grouped chunks rather than as one large number.
examples: 0 nula, 1 jedna, 2 dva, 3 tři, 4 čtyři, 5 pět
